# MEMEX — Personal Memory & Automation Stack for Claude

A distribution-ready setup guide for the infrastructure that backs Artem's shared
memory across every Claude surface (Chat, Code, iPad, Cowork, Telegram). You can
clone it, plug in your own secrets, and run it on your own Cloudflare account.

**Status:** Production on `a-papilov` Cloudflare account since 2026-03-12. Covers
42 MCP tools, one Telegram bot, daily cron, CI/CD, hooks, and a nightly
dreaming protocol. Last-reviewed tool matrix on 2026-04-20.

> If you just want to understand the *architecture*, read `CLAUDE.md` instead.
> This guide is for setting it up on your own infrastructure.

---

## Table of contents

1. [Prerequisites](#1-prerequisites)
2. [Quick start (20 min)](#2-quick-start-20-min)
3. [Tool catalog (15 tools)](#3-tool-catalog)

6. [Verifying a deploy](#6-verifying-a-deploy)
7. [Staying in sync](#7-staying-in-sync)

---

## 1. Prerequisites

You will need:

- **Cloudflare account** (free tier is enough to start). Workers + D1 + R2.
- **GitHub account** + `gh` CLI authenticated (`gh auth login`).
- **Anthropic API key** (for the Telegram bot — the MCP worker does not call
  Claude directly, it's tool-side).
- **Telegram Bot token** — only if you want the TG bot.
- **Granola Pro** — only if you want meeting sync.
- **Node.js 20+** and `npx wrangler`.
- One machine where you will use Claude Code (Mac/Linux; Mac recommended for the
  optional Apple Calendar / Notes integrations).

This stack is **single-user by design**. The allowlist pattern in the TG bot
and the hard-coded chat_id routing assume one primary user. If you multi-tenant
it, expect to touch the allowlist and per-user counters in several places.

---

## 2. Quick start (20 min)

Clone, rename, deploy, wire. This gets you the MCP worker talking to a private
GitHub repo as a memory store — the minimum viable surface. The TG bot and
Google connectors are additive on top.

```bash
# 1. Fork + clone
gh repo create your-user/my-memory --private --clone
cd my-memory
# Copy the skeleton we ship in this repo:
curl -L https://github.com/a-pap/claude-memory/archive/main.tar.gz | tar xz --strip-components=1

# 2. Rewrite hub content to your life
#    (see hubs/README.md for the file shape)
$EDITOR CLAUDE.md STATUS_SNAPSHOT.md hubs/02_personal_profile.md

# 3. Provision Cloudflare primitives
cd config/mcp-worker
npx wrangler d1 create claude-memory-db          # copy the id
npx wrangler r2 bucket create claude-memory-store # or reuse
$EDITOR wrangler.toml  # paste d1 id, confirm bucket name

# 4. Set secrets (ONE TIME, never commit)
CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN=...  # with Workers + D1 + R2 edit scopes
npx wrangler secret put GITHUB_PAT        # PAT with repo:read on YOUR memory repo
npx wrangler secret put AUTH_PATH_TOKEN   # openssl rand -hex 32
npx wrangler secret put GRANOLA_API       # optional, grn_... bearer token

# 5. Deploy
npm ci && npx wrangler deploy

# 6. Verify
curl -X POST https://claude-memory-mcp.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}'
# Expect 42 tools in the result.

# 7. Connect to claude.ai
# Settings → Connectors → Add custom → paste the /mcp URL.
# Or in Claude Code: add to ~/.claude/settings.json under mcpServers.
```

That's it for the MCP worker. Add the TG bot (§3.2), hooks (§3.6), and CI
(§3.4) as you go — each is ~15 minutes.

---

## 3. Tool catalog

15 tools, grouped by layer. Each section: **what / why / setup / verify**.
Tools marked *optional* can be skipped; the core (§3.1 + §3.4 + §3.5) is the
minimum to be useful.

### 3.1 `claude-memory-mcp` worker  (core)

**What.** A Cloudflare Worker exposing 42 MCP tools + 3 MCP prompts over
JSON-RPC at `/mcp`. Backed by D1 (facts, sessions, errors, knowledge graph,
Granola cache) and R2 (conversation backups, large uploads).

**Why.** Central tool layer for every Claude surface. Read/write memory from
Chat, Code, iPad, TG — all via the same API. Single source of truth.

**Setup.**

1. D1 database:
   ```bash
   npx wrangler d1 create claude-memory-db
   # paste UUID into config/mcp-worker/wrangler.toml → [[d1_databases]] → database_id
   ```
2. R2 bucket:
   ```bash
   npx wrangler r2 bucket create claude-memory-store
   # wrangler.toml already references it by name
   ```
3. Secrets (see §4 for full reference):
   ```bash
   cd config/mcp-worker
   npx wrangler secret put GITHUB_PAT
   npx wrangler secret put AUTH_PATH_TOKEN
   npx wrangler secret put GRANOLA_API          # optional
   ```
4. Deploy:
   ```bash
   npm ci
   CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN=... npx wrangler deploy
   ```
5. Connect it:
   - **Claude Chat / iPad**: Settings → Connectors → Add custom → URL =
     `https://claude-memory-mcp.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/mcp` (OAuth-less — the
     open `/mcp` path is intentional for the Chat connector; the token-gated
     `/mcp/{AUTH_PATH_TOKEN}` is for `curl` from scripts).
   - **Claude Code**: add to `~/.claude/settings.json` under `mcpServers`.

**Verify.**

```bash
curl -X POST https://YOUR-WORKER.workers.dev/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}' \
  | jq '.result.tools | length'
# → 42
```

---

### 3.2 `claude-telegram-bot` worker

**What.** A second Cloudflare Worker that ingests every message forwarded to
a personal Telegram bot, persists it to D1 + R2, and optionally replies via
Claude Opus 4.7 (only on explicit triggers — silent save by default).

**Why.** Fastest possible capture surface. Forward an article, a GIF, a PDF,
a voice note, or a throwaway thought — it lands in your memory with a 👀
reaction and zero cognitive overhead. Ask an explicit question (`?` or
imperative verb) and Claude answers with full memory context.

**Setup.** The dedicated README has the full procedure — follow it:

👉 **[`config/telegram-bot/README.md`](config/telegram-bot/README.md)** (self-contained, 150 lines).

Short version:

1. `@BotFather` → new bot, save token, disable privacy.
2. Send one message to the bot, grab your numeric chat_id from
   `https://api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/getUpdates`.
3. `openssl rand -hex 32` → webhook secret.
4. `cd config/telegram-bot && npx wrangler secret put TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`
   (plus `TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `MEMORY_MCP_TOKEN`).
5. `$EDITOR wrangler.toml` → set `ALLOWED_CHAT_IDS` to your chat_id.
6. `npx wrangler deploy`.
7. Register the webhook:
   ```bash
   curl "https://api.telegram.org/bot$TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN/setWebhook?\
     url=https://claude-telegram-bot.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/tg/$TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_SECRET&\
     secret_token=$TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_SECRET"
   ```

**Verify.** Message the bot in Telegram. Expect a 👀 reaction within ~2s. If
you typed a question ending with `?`, expect a full Claude reply within ~10s.

**Rotate the webhook secret.** `config/telegram-bot/rotate-webhook-secret.sh`
— idempotent, updates both `wrangler secret` stores and calls `setWebhook`.
Run every ~90 days or if you suspect compromise.

---

### 3.3 Cron triggers

**What.** Two cron schedules, one per worker.
- `claude-memory-mcp`: daily 07:00 UTC → self-diagnostic (STATUS_SNAPSHOT
  freshness, D1 health, GitHub API reachability) + Granola sync (last 2 days)
  + heartbeat to D1 `sessions`.
- `claude-telegram-bot`: every 5 min → reclaims stuck 'processing' rows,
  retries 'queued'/'failed' rows with `retry_count < 3`.

**Why.** Catches drift between sessions. Memory that isn't automatically
reconciled decays — the daily cron is the reconciliation. The 5-min TG sweep
means no message is lost even if the worker crashes mid-processing.

**Setup.** Already declared in each worker's `wrangler.toml`:

```toml
# config/mcp-worker/wrangler.toml
[triggers]
crons = ["0 7 * * *"]

# config/telegram-bot/wrangler.toml
[triggers]
crons = ["*/5 * * * *"]
```

No manual action beyond `wrangler deploy`. Cloudflare wires them up
automatically.

**Verify.** After 24h, check:

```bash
npx wrangler tail claude-memory-mcp | grep cron
# or query D1:
npx wrangler d1 execute claude-memory-db --remote \
  --command "SELECT * FROM sessions WHERE source='cron' ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5"
```

---

### 3.4 GitHub Actions CI/CD

**What.** Auto-deploy both workers on push to `main`. Gates on `tsc --noEmit`,
structural tests (`tests/run-all.sh`), and secret scan. Auto-merges Claude-
authored PRs when checks pass.

**Why.** Every Claude surface (Chat, Code, Cowork, TG) can push to this repo.
Without CI they would deploy broken code in ~days. With CI the deploy is
gated and safe.

**Workflows shipped:**

| File | Trigger | Does |
|------|---------|------|
| `.github/workflows/deploy-mcp.yml` | push main, touches `config/mcp-worker/**` | typecheck → wrangler deploy |
| `.github/workflows/deploy-telegram-bot.yml` | push main, touches `config/telegram-bot/**` | typecheck → wrangler deploy |
| `.github/workflows/test-structure.yml` | push main + every PR | runs `tests/run-all.sh` |
| `.github/workflows/lint-secrets.yml` | push main + every PR | regex scan for `cfut_|ghp_|grn_|sk-|[0-9a-f]{60,}` |
| `.github/workflows/auto-merge.yml` | PR open + checks pass | squash-merge Claude PRs |

**Setup.**

1. `Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions` in your fork. Add:
   - `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` — Workers+D1+R2 edit scope (not Global API key!).
   - `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID`.
   - `GRANOLA_API` (if using Granola).
2. Ensure Actions are enabled in repo settings.
3. On first push: watch Actions tab, expect green.

**Verify.** After a push that touches a worker, open Actions → Deploy
{MCP,Telegram} Bot Worker → should complete in ~1 min with `success`.

---

### 3.5 Pre-commit secret scanner

**What.** `config/hooks/pre-commit-secret-scan.sh` — blocks `git commit` if
the diff contains anything matching `cfut_`, `ghp_`, `gho_`, `github_pat_`,
`grn_`, `sk-`, or `[0-9a-f]{60,}`. Runs before the commit object is written.

**Why.** We had a real incident on 2026-04-10 where a token leaked into a
commit and was pushed to public. This hook is the local-side half of the
defense; `.github/workflows/lint-secrets.yml` is the server-side half. Both
must pass.

**Setup.**

```bash
bash config/hooks/install-pre-commit.sh
```

That script:
1. Copies the hook to `.git/hooks/pre-commit`.
2. Chains existing hooks (so you don't clobber husky/lefthook setups).

**Verify.**

```bash
# Put any real-shaped PAT pattern in a file (here we just describe it —
# typing a literal 'ghp_' + 40 chars into this doc would trip the scanner
# on itself):
printf 'ghp_%s\n' "$(openssl rand -hex 20)" > /tmp/evil.txt
git add /tmp/evil.txt
git commit -m "should fail"
# → pre-commit rejects the commit with ⛔ POTENTIAL SECRET DETECTED
```

Per-incident log + policy is in `SECURITY.md`.

---

### 3.6 Session hooks (Start + End)

**What.** Two shell hooks wired into `~/.claude/settings.json` via the Claude
Code `hooks.{SessionStart,SessionEnd}` arrays.

- `SessionStart` → `config/hooks/session-start.sh`: `git pull` the memory
  repo so the first turn sees fresh data; prints a one-line staleness
  warning if STATUS_SNAPSHOT is >14 days unfreshed or the latest dreaming
  summary is >7 days old.
- `SessionEnd` → `config/hooks/auto-log-session.sh`: writes a row to
  `sessions` D1 table via the MCP `auto_log` tool (token counts, duration,
  ended_reason).

**Why.** Zero-friction reconciliation. You never have to remember to pull or
log — it just happens.

**Setup.**

```bash
$EDITOR ~/.claude/settings.json
```

Add:

```json
"hooks": {
  "SessionStart": [
    {
      "matcher": "",
      "hooks": [
        { "type": "command", "command": "bash /ABS/PATH/TO/claude-memory/config/hooks/session-start.sh" }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "SessionEnd": [
    {
      "matcher": "",
      "hooks": [
        { "type": "command", "command": "bash /ABS/PATH/TO/claude-memory/config/hooks/auto-log-session.sh" }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Absolute paths only — `~` is not expanded by the harness.

**Verify.** Start a new Claude Code session — if the memory repo had
unpulled commits, you'll see `[session-start] Pulled memory updates: ...` in
the terminal banner. End the session normally and query D1:

```bash
npx wrangler d1 execute claude-memory-db --remote \
  --command "SELECT * FROM sessions ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 3"
```

You should see the auto-logged row.

---

### 3.7 Skills + plugins

**What.** Claude Code plugins from official + community marketplaces,
enabled via `~/.claude/settings.json` → `enabledPlugins`. Typical set:
`superpowers`, `skill-creator`, `code-review`, `claude-md-management`,
`episodic-memory`, `cloudflare`, plus whatever you use for your own domain.

**Why.** Skills are how the CLI actually does structured work — brainstorm,
plan, execute, review, commit — without you having to describe the process
every time.

**Setup.**

1. Install plugins:
   ```bash
   /plugin install superpowers@superpowers-marketplace
   /plugin install skill-creator@claude-plugins-official
   /plugin install cloudflare@claude-plugins-official
   # etc. — see ~/.claude/settings.json for Artem's list
   ```
2. Verify in a session: `/help` → skills list should include the installed ones.

**Verify.** Invoke a skill: `/brainstorm product idea X` — if the skill
loads, you'll see its structured flow.

Personal skills (specific to your workflow) go in `skills/` in this repo.
They're auto-discovered when the memory repo is the working directory.

---

### 3.8 Dreaming protocol

**What.** A nightly Claude Code session triggered by `/schedule` that audits
memory health, archives yesterday's changes, and writes a short dream entry.
Full protocol is in `config/DREAMING_TASK.md` (~40KB — detailed Phase 0-6
procedure).

**Why.** Humans do memory consolidation during sleep; this infra does the
analogous cycle nightly. Without it, memory drifts in ~a week: stale
deadlines, open TODOs never closed, hub content not merged with fresh facts.

**Setup.**

1. On a machine that runs overnight (typically your work laptop):
   ```bash
   /schedule "0 3 * * *" "Read config/DREAMING_TASK.md and execute the LIGHT cycle (Mon-Sat) or BURN cycle (Thu)"
   ```
2. Verify the scheduled task landed: `/schedule list`.
3. First run: wait for 03:00 local, then check `logs/dreaming/` — there
   should be a dated summary file.

**Verify.** In the morning:

```bash
ls -t logs/dreaming/*_summary.md | head -1
# expect today's date
```

The `dreaming_status` MCP tool also returns FRESH/OK/STALE/BROKEN + age:

```json
{"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"dreaming_status","arguments":{}}}
```

---

### 3.9 Cowork scheduled tasks

**What.** `claude.ai/code/scheduled` (Cowork UI) lets you run Claude tasks
on a schedule with a dedicated container. We use one such task: daily 09:00
CET memory sync — pull recent chats, calendar, reminders, commit to repo.
Protocol: `config/COWORK_SYNC_TASK.md`.

**Why.** Cowork has access to surfaces Code/Chat don't (root-scope chats,
Apple Calendar on the linked Mac). Running the sync there fills the daily
gap that SessionStart + cron can't.

**Setup.**

1. Open `claude.ai/code/scheduled`.
2. New task:
   - **Schedule**: `0 7 * * *` UTC (= 09:00 CET).
   - **Prompt**: paste the full body of `config/COWORK_SYNC_TASK.md`.
   - **Env vars**: `GITHUB_PAT` (repo write scope).
3. Save + run once manually to verify.

**Verify.** After the first morning run:

```bash
git log --author="Claude (Cowork Sync)" --oneline | head -3
```

Should show a `daily sync:` commit from ~09:00 CET.

---

### 3.10 Granola sync

**What.** Pulls meeting summaries (AI-generated) from Granola's REST API
into D1 `granola_meetings` nightly via the MCP worker cron.

**Why.** Meeting context is the most time-lossy memory — you forget who said
what within a week. Automatic sync + searchable D1 means Claude can always
answer "что Олег сказал про X на последнем weekly" without you reopening
Granola.

**Setup.**

1. Granola Pro required.
2. Get the API token: Granola → Settings → API → copy the `grn_...` Bearer.
3. Install in the MCP worker:
   ```bash
   cd config/mcp-worker
   npx wrangler secret put GRANOLA_API    # paste grn_...
   ```
4. Also add `GRANOLA_API` to GitHub Actions secrets (for CI/CD).
5. Redeploy: `npx wrangler deploy`.
6. First sync (manual backfill):
   ```bash
   curl -X POST https://claude-memory-mcp.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/mcp/$AUTH_PATH_TOKEN \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
     -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"granola_sync","arguments":{"days_back":30}}}'
   ```

**Verify.**

```bash
# Recent meetings are in D1:
npx wrangler d1 execute claude-memory-db --remote \
  --command "SELECT title, date, domain FROM granola_meetings ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT 5"
# Via MCP:
curl ... -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"granola_recent","arguments":{"limit":5}}}'
```

Rate limits: 25 req/5s burst, 5 req/s sustained. The cron stays well under.

---

### 3.11 Gmail connector

**What.** claude.ai managed connector. Gives Claude Chat (and claude.ai
Projects) read access to your Gmail — threads, drafts, labels, search.

**Why.** Most people's "who said what" lives in email. Without this, Claude
can't answer "what did the lawyer send about the residencia?"

**Setup.**

1. claude.ai → Settings → Connectors → Gmail → Connect.
2. OAuth consent — read-only scope is enough.
3. Verify in Chat: "List my last 5 emails" — should return real subjects.

**Scope.** Read-only by default. Claude cannot send or delete mail without
you explicitly consenting to a richer scope (which we don't recommend —
drafts + search is 99% of the value).

**No setup on this repo's side.** Everything lives in claude.ai.

---

### 3.12 Google Drive connector

**What.** claude.ai managed connector. Search + read Google Docs / Sheets /
PDFs stored in your Drive.

**Why.** Long-form artifacts (planning docs, legal PDFs, spreadsheets)
outgrow chat context. Drive is where they live; this makes them
addressable.

**Setup.**

1. claude.ai → Settings → Connectors → Google Drive → Connect.
2. Consent scope: *all-my-files* is overkill — pick folder-scoped if the
   UI offers it, or create a dedicated `claude-readable/` folder and only
   share that.
3. Verify in Chat: "search drive for 'residencia'" — should surface your
   real docs.

**No setup on this repo's side.** claude.ai-only.

---

### 3.13 Apple Calendar (Mac only)

**What.** Claude Chat on macOS can query the local Calendar.app database
when the Claude Desktop extension is installed. Reads events — does not
modify. *Only available on the Mac where Claude Desktop runs.*

**Why.** Schedule context is high-value and low-latency. Planning for
Monday, asking "what's on my calendar tomorrow" — no need to open Cal.app.

**Setup.**

1. Install Claude Desktop from claude.ai/download.
2. First query triggers a macOS permission prompt — approve Calendar access.
3. Verify in Chat: "what's on my calendar today?"

**If you don't use Claude Desktop**, skip. No server-side equivalent — the
Cowork daily sync (§3.9) can export a 7-day calendar snapshot manually, but
that's less live.

---

### 3.14 Apple Notes (Mac only)

**What.** Same pattern as §3.13 but for Notes.app.

**Why.** Most people's non-Drive scratch notes live here. Access is
optional but convenient.

**Setup.** Install Claude Desktop, grant permission on first query.

**Verify.** "search Apple Notes for 'relocation'" — should return matching
note titles and excerpts.

---

### 3.15 R2 lifecycle

**What.** `config/telegram-bot/r2-lifecycle-setup.sh` — sets a 365-day
lifecycle rule on the `telegram/` prefix in the `claude-memory-store` R2
bucket, so Telegram media archives prune automatically.

**Why.** Without a lifecycle rule, every forwarded GIF lives forever and R2
storage cost climbs linearly. 365d is long enough that "what was that
meme" works; shorter is too aggressive.

**Setup.**

```bash
cd config/telegram-bot
./r2-lifecycle-setup.sh
# needs CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN + account id in env
```

The script is idempotent — safe to re-run.

**Verify.**

```bash
# Cloudflare dashboard → R2 → claude-memory-store → Settings → Lifecycle
# Expect rule: "Delete telegram/** after 365d"
```

---

## 4. Secrets reference

**Never commit any of these.** The pre-commit hook (§3.5) blocks the common
token shapes; the repo's `SECURITY.md` documents the 2026-04-10 incident
and the defense-in-depth response.

| Secret | Where used | Scope needed | Rotation cadence |
|--------|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` | Local `npx wrangler` + GitHub Actions | Workers + D1 + R2 edit | 90 days |
| `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` | GitHub Actions | Not a secret, but set once | — |
| `GITHUB_PAT` | MCP worker (secret), Cowork task env | `repo:read` + `repo:write` on memory repo | 90 days |
| `AUTH_PATH_TOKEN` | MCP worker secret | `openssl rand -hex 32` | 180 days |
| `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` | TG bot worker secret | From `@BotFather` | On compromise only |
| `TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_SECRET` | TG bot worker secret + webhook URL + header | `openssl rand -hex 32` | 90 days; use `rotate-webhook-secret.sh` |
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | TG bot worker secret | From console.anthropic.com | 180 days |
| `MEMORY_MCP_TOKEN` | TG bot worker secret | Matches `AUTH_PATH_TOKEN` on MCP side | Same as `AUTH_PATH_TOKEN` |
| `GRANOLA_API` | MCP worker secret + GitHub Actions | From Granola Settings → API | On compromise only |

Helpful one-liners:

```bash
# Full key check list (expect all to exist):
cd config/mcp-worker && npx wrangler secret list
cd ../telegram-bot && npx wrangler secret list

# Scan repo for anything secret-shaped:
bash config/hooks/pre-commit-secret-scan.sh $(git ls-files)
```

---

## 5. Troubleshooting

### MCP worker returns 404

- Path — use `/mcp` (for Chat connector) or `/mcp/{AUTH_PATH_TOKEN}` (for
  scripted calls). Anything else 404s.
- Account — you probably deployed to the wrong Cloudflare account. Check
  `wrangler whoami`.

### TG bot is silent after a forward

- Check it's actually deployed: `curl /health` → `ok`.
- Check D1 `telegram_messages` for the row. If status='queued' forever, the
  cron sweep isn't running — check `wrangler tail` for the scheduled event.
- Reaction emoji didn't land? The chat may have reactions disabled. The bot
  falls back to sending `"запомнил"` text — expect that instead.
- Bot replies with `⚠️ thinking.type.enabled` → you're on old code.
  Redeploy from `main`, this was fixed in PR #76 (2026-04-20).

### "1042" / hallucinated tool errors

The bot used to invent HTTP 1042/404 errors when a tool genuinely failed,
then pretend the infra was down. Fixed structurally 2026-04-18 — errors are
now prepended verbatim with `⚠️ TOOL ERROR (verbatim):` in code, not
prompt. Still seeing hallucinated errors? Check `reply_status='hallucinated'`
in `telegram_messages` — old rows are quarantined but new ones shouldn't
appear.

### Cron never runs

- `wrangler.toml` has `[triggers] crons = [...]`? — yes, shipped.
- Cloudflare paid plan? Crons run on free tier too, but have per-account
  limits on the number of schedules. Check the dashboard.
- `wrangler tail` during the expected window to confirm the `scheduled`
  handler fires.

### Secret scan blocks a legit commit

- Check the regex in `config/hooks/pre-commit-secret-scan.sh`. If a long
  hex string is genuinely not a secret (an IPFS hash, a git SHA), wrap it
  in a string the hook tolerates — or edit the regex for a one-off.
- Never pass `--no-verify` to bypass without fixing the root cause.

### Granola rows aren't showing up

- `wrangler secret list` → is `GRANOLA_API` set?
- Token shape: starts with `grn_`. If it doesn't, you pasted the wrong
  thing.
- First sync after a 30-day gap can hit the rate limit — retry with
  `days_back: 7` first, then 30.

### Two Cloudflare Workers, same D1 — which one runs the migration?

The MCP worker owns the memory schema (facts, sessions, errors, kg,
granola). The TG bot owns its own table `telegram_messages` and ALTERs it
idempotently in its own `ensureTable()`. The two never CREATE the same
table. If you see a column drift error, it's almost always because you ran
a manual `wrangler d1 execute` without syncing. Prefer code-side migrations
inside `ensureTable()` — idempotent ALTERs with duplicate-column catch.

---

## 6. Verifying a deploy

Canonical post-deploy smoke test. Run it after any merge to `main` that
touches `config/`:

```bash
# 1. MCP worker — tool count + prompt count
curl -X POST https://claude-memory-mcp.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}' \
  | jq '.result.tools | length'
# → 42

curl -X POST https://claude-memory-mcp.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/mcp \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"prompts/list","params":{}}' \
  | jq '.result.prompts | length'
# → 3

# 2. TG bot health
curl https://claude-telegram-bot.YOUR-ACCT.workers.dev/health
# → ok

# 3. D1 tables present
npx wrangler d1 execute claude-memory-db --remote \
  --command "SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name"
# → expect: facts, sessions, errors, knowledge_graph, granola_meetings, telegram_messages

# 4. Structural tests
bash tests/run-all.sh
# → "Summary: N passed, 0 failed"

# 5. Typecheck both workers
(cd config/mcp-worker && npx tsc --noEmit) && (cd config/telegram-bot && npx tsc --noEmit) && echo "typecheck ok"
```

`VERIFICATION_CHECKLIST.md` has the longer version with screenshots and D1
row counts for full reincarnation after a wipe.

---

## 7. Staying in sync

This stack is a living infra. Each surface can push code; bugs land faster
than docs. The reconciliation strategy has three layers:

1. **CI-gated deploys** (§3.4) — broken code doesn't reach production.
2. **Daily cron self-diagnostic** (§3.3) — silent drift surfaces as D1
   heartbeat gaps.
3. **Nightly dreaming protocol** (§3.8) — weekly hub content + status
   reconciliation.

If all three are running, the lag between "the infra changed" and "the docs
reflect it" is bounded to ~24h (daily sync) for facts and ~7d (weekly BURN
cycle) for architecture.

When you change something substantial:
- Update `CLAUDE.md` §MCP if you add/rename a tool.
- Update `STATUS_SNAPSHOT.md` §MCP Infrastructure for version/tool-count.
- Update `references/mcp_tools_audit_2026_04.md` with a new row + signal +
  verdict.
- Update this file (`MEMEX_GUIDE.md`) for any surface-visible change.
- Run `bash tests/run-all.sh` before committing.

The structural tests enforce some of this automatically — `tool_count_matches_docs`
will fail CI if the three Markdown files disagree on tool count.

---

## License / sharing

This setup is an opinionated personal stack. Fork it, rewrite the hub
content, rotate the secrets, and own it yourself. There is no central
service you'd depend on — every component either runs on your own
Cloudflare account or is a managed connector you OAuth into individually.
If you hit a genuine bug in the shipped code (not your config), PRs
welcome on the upstream repo.
